profile.summary: Owns the quality and direction of UX across a product area or team. Balances hands-on design work with coaching designers, shaping process, and aligning product, engineering, and research around user needs. Often sets design standards, drives critique, and helps the team turn messy problems into usable, tested experiences.

known_challenges.text: Balancing personal design output with coaching, reviews, and cross-functional alignment work.
Protecting research, usability, and accessibility quality when delivery pressure pushes for speed.
Giving clear design direction without becoming a bottleneck for every decision.
Carrying the emotional load of constant feedback, critique, and stakeholder disagreement.
Translating broad product goals into a coherent user experience across teams and touchpoints.

recommended_question_themes.why: This role often has to defend user experience standards while still helping the team ship on time.
A lead succeeds by growing other designers and creating clarity, not by doing all the important work personally.
Much of the role depends on getting product and engineering to align on user needs and design trade-offs.
Leads absorb critique, unblock others, and handle conflict, which can quietly drain energy over time.
They are usually responsible for connecting individual features into a coherent experience across the product area.

terminology.meaning: Structured review of work to improve decisions and quality.
The steps a user takes to complete a task.
Low-detail layout showing structure and interaction.
Clickable model used to test or explain ideas.
Designing so people with disabilities can use it.
Ongoing learning from users during product development.
Checking work for quality, consistency, and readiness.
Getting key people to agree on direction and trade-offs.
Handing off ownership with enough context and trust.
How the team gives, receives, and uses critique.
Choosing what matters most with limited time.
Shared components and rules for consistent product design.
Early work to understand problems, users, and options.
Passing design decisions and assets to engineering.
UX issues left unresolved that slow future work.
Watching users try tasks to find friction.
Using AI tools to speed up early design exploration.

listen_for: Whether they talk about team outcomes or only their own design output.
language that shows they are stuck being the approval point for too many decisions
whether they can name user evidence behind design choices or rely mostly on opinion
signs they are shielding the team from stakeholder churn without enough support
shifts from craft language to resourcing, alignment, and prioritisation concerns
avoid: Treating them like a pure people manager with no need for hands-on design credibility.
Pushing them to move faster without discussing research, accessibility, or design quality trade-offs.
Assuming stakeholder conflict means they are not being collaborative enough.
Using them as the default fixer for every design problem on the team.
